coredata.dc:title: Bright and multicolor emissive carbon dots/organosilicon composite for highly efficient tandem luminescent solar concentrators
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yuhan
Zhao, Liuxi
Cao, Xiyue
Zhang, Yuhong
Jiang, Xuanfeng
Sun, Zhengguang
Zhan, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) hold great promise for effective alternatives to traditional luminescent materials in the field of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) due to their low cost, eco-friendly and stability. How to improve the performance of CDs based LSCs is the research focus at present. Herein, a novel CDs/organosilicon composite (SiCDs) with tunable solid-state fluorescence and photoluminescence quantum yield from 21% to 56% is reported to cope with the limitation of aggregation-caused quenching of CDs on the performance improvement of LSCs. The emission redshift mechanism of SiCDs have been explored in detail. Furthermore, the UV absorption of SiCDs is in wide range and gradual increase in intensity as the emission red shifted from blue to orange. The SiCDs are further designed to construct a series of transparent single-layer and tandem LSCs with polydimethylsiloxane as waveguide material, and the tandem LSC based on blue and yellow emission SiCDs achieve a finally external optical efficiency of 7.10%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Organosilicon composites
Tunable solid-state fluorescence
Luminescent solar concentrators
Tandem

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of SiCDs composites with multicolor emission
Fabrication of LSCs based on multicolor emissive SiCDs composites
Evaluation of the performance of the LSC devices

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Solvothermal strategy for synthesis of multicolor fluorescent SiCDs and the corresponding optical photograph of the SiCDs powder from blue to orange under natural light and 365 nm UV lamp. b) PL emission spectra of the SiCDs composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FESEM images of a) B-SiCDs, b) G-SiCDs, c) Y-SiCDs, and d) O-SiCDs. HRTEM images of e) B-SiCDs, f) G-SiCDs, g) Y-SiCDs, and h) O-SiCDs (The insets are the corresponding single CDs and particle size distribution diagrams, respectively). i) P-XRD pattern and j) FT-IR spectra of the four composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Excitation-emission matrix for a) B-SiCDs, b) G-SiCDs, c) Y-SiCDs, and d) O-SiCDs. Absorption, PLE and PL spectra of e) B-SiCDs, f) G-SiCDs, g) Y-SiCDs, and h) O-SiCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical photograph of diluted solution of B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs after etching under a1-d1) day light and a2-d2) 365 nm UV light. e) PL emission spectra of etching solution of B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs after diluting 5, 20, 80 and 320 times, respectively. Optical photograph of diluted solution of B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs (0.2 mg mL−1) under f1-i1) day light and f2-i2) 365 nm UV light. j) PL emission spectra of diluted solution of B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Photograph of B-, G-, Y, and O-LSC under natural light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). b) transmittance spectra, c) absorption spectra and d) PL emission spectra of B-, G-, Y, and O-LSC. e) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J–V) of single-layer LSCs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Schematic illustration for the fabrication of tandem LSCs. b) B-LSCs stacked on Y-LSCs under 365 nm UV lamp. c) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J–V) of tandem LSCs. d) The corresponding external optical efficiencies histogram of tandem LSCs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram for the formation mechanism of a) B-SiCDs, b) G-SiCDs, c) Y-SiCDs and d) O-SiCDs.

head.ce:title: Bright and multicolor emissive carbon dots/organosilicon composite for highly efficient tandem luminescent solar concentrators

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) hold great promise for effective alternatives to traditional luminescent materials in the field of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) due to their low cost, eco-friendly and stability. How to improve the performance of CDs based LSCs is the research focus at present. Herein, a novel CDs/organosilicon composite (SiCDs) with tunable solid-state fluorescence and photoluminescence quantum yield from 21% to 56% is reported to cope with the limitation of aggregation-caused quenching of CDs on the performance improvement of LSCs. The emission redshift mechanism of SiCDs have been explored in detail. Furthermore, the UV absorption of SiCDs is in wide range and gradual increase in intensity as the emission red shifted from blue to orange. The SiCDs are further designed to construct a series of transparent single-layer and tandem LSCs with polydimethylsiloxane as waveguide material, and the tandem LSC based on blue and yellow emission SiCDs achieve a finally external optical efficiency of 7.10%.

Carbon dot/organosilicon (SiCDs) composites with solid-state fluorescence from blue to orange were synthesized by simple solvothermal method. The emission and structure of SiCDs can be controlled just by varying the molar ratio of the ingredient. The SiCDs composites show good compatibility with the self-made polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), and their loaded content can reach 15 wt% accompanied with negligible variation of emission wavelength and photoluminescence quantum yields. The SiCDs and PDMS are further applied to construct a series of transparent single-layer and tandem LSCs, in which the tandem LSCs based on blue and yellow emission SiCDs have a finally external optical efficiency of 7.10%.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Harnessing solar energy for electricity generation holds great promise to solve current resource and environmental issues [1,2]. Among sunlight concentration devices, luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are attracting increasing attention due to their adjustable structure, sustainable use and promise of cost reduction [3]. A typical LSC is usually composed of waveguide materials and luminescent materials, where the luminescent material is used to absorb the solar photons and then re-emit the low-energy photons that will reach the edge-coupled cells through total internal reflection in the waveguide materials, thus realizing the collection of solar energy [4]. To date, a variety kind of luminescent materials such as organic dyes [5–7], semiconductor quantum dots [8–10], and perovskite nanocrystals [11,12] have been used to construct LSCs. Compared with the above luminescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) not only have the characteristics of adjustable absorption and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQYs), but also have the advantages of good photostability, low toxicity, low preparation cost, and a wide range of precursors [13–15], thus experiencing increasing attention in the field of LSCs during the past few years [16–18].

At present, the research focus of CDs based LSCs is mainly on improving their efficiency to achieve the standard of practical usability. It is well known that the aggregation-caused quenching effect of CDs severely limits their performance in solid-state devices. In the field of LSCs, this problem is embodied in the severely limited dispersion concentration of CDs in waveguide materials (usually less than 2 wt%), which will undoubtedly hinder the performance improvement of CDs-based LSCs [19]. Previously, our team designed a yellow-green emissive carbon dot/organosilicon composite with the PLQY of 49% to prepare single-layer LSC with a maximum external optical efficiency of 5.89% [20]. However, the working range of single-layer LSCs is mainly concentrated in the UV-blue light region, suffering from limited spectral coverage on the solar spectrum. Therefore, in addition to the optimization of luminescent materials, single-layer LSCs that respond to different sunlight bands can be connected in tandem structure to realize structural optimization, which is also an effective strategy to improve the overall performance of LSCs. To date, tandem LSCs that combined CDs with other luminescent elements, such as aggregation-induced emission molecules [21], CDs [22,23], dyes [24] and perovskite QDs [25] have been developed and achieved great progress. However, different synthetic materials, synthetic methods and purification methods of these luminescent materials increase the preparation cost of LSCs. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a kind of multicolor solid-state fluorescent CDs that can be used to construct tandem LSCs to improve the performance of LSCs and reduce the preparation cost of LSCs, for realizing the transformation of LSCs from theory to practice.

In this paper, multicolor emissive carbon dot/organosilicon (SiCDs) composites were prepared by solvothermal method using citric acid (CA), urea and γ-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as precursor. By adjusting the ratio of urea to CA and APTES to CA, the emission of the SiCDs can be regulated from blue to orange. More importantly, the absorption intensity of the composites also appears to increase with the red shift of the emission, which provides the feasibility for the construction of tandem LSCs. The SiCDs composites exhibit good compatibility with the self-made polydimethylsiloxane to prepare transparent single-layer LSCs by spin-coating method. With further combination with blue and yellow emission SiCDs-based LSCs, the tandem LSC exhibited a high external optical efficiency of 7.10%. This work not only provides a new strategy for the preparation of CDs-based materials with tunable solid-state fluorescence, but also improves the way for the construction of efficient tandem LSCs.

Citrate acid (CA, ≥99.5%), urea (99%) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co, Ltd. Ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.7%) was purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40%) was purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS, with the polymerization degree of approximately 200) was homemade in the laboratory (preparation method and related characterization are in the Supporting Information of Figs. S1–S4). Tyzor®722 titanate chelate used as catalyst was obtained from Guangzhou Jianyi chemicals Imp & Exp Co, Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SiCDs composites with multicolor emission
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2.7 mmol CA, a certain amount of urea (0 mmol, 43.2 mmol or 86.4 mmol), and 16.2 mmol APTES were dissolved in the mixture of 10 mL H2O and 20 mL EtOH and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. The reactants were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and then placed in a constant temperature air-blast drying oven at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, precipitates were collected by centrifuging and washed by water and ethanol respectively for three times, and then dried overnight at 50 °C in a vacuum to obtain blue, green, or yellow emissive composites. The orange emissive SiCDs composites were obtained as the same preparation conditions as yellow emissive composites just by further reducing the content of APTES to 5.4 mmol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of LSCs based on multicolor emissive SiCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: First, the dried SiCDs powder was thoroughly ground and screened with a 600-mesh screen. Secondly, the sifted powder was mixed with self-made PDMS in a certain mass fraction (Detailed recipes are listed in Table S1) and stirred in a 5 mL beaker with magnetic stirrers for 4 h to ensure that the powder was fully mixed with PDMS. Then a certain amount of Tyzor®722 titanate chelate as catalyst was added into the mixture and continue to stir for 5 min. Finally, the mixture was rotated on a glass substrate of 25 × 25 × 1 mm3 with a gluing machine at a speed of 2000 revolutions per minute, and cured at room temperature for 48 h to obtain a uniform film.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were conducted on a JEOL JEM 2100F electron microscope. The morphology of the SiCDs was observed with a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Sigma 500, Zeiss, Germany) under an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. Crystallographic information was obtained on powder X-ray diffraction (P-XRD) equipped with a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Cu ΚR radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å)). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was implemented by a Ther-mofisher Nicolet iS50 Spectrometer using KBr pellets with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer. The solid-state 13C nuclear magnetic resonance measurements were performed on BRUKER AVANCE 400 for 100.6 MHz 13C. The fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PicoQuant Fluo Time 300). The PLQY were measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere and the test was repeated three times for each sample. The fluorescence lifetime curves were measured on fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 420 nm laser. The UV–visible absorption and transmission spectra were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu Corp, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the performance of the LSC devices
ce:section.ce:para:
The current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the silicon solar cells were measured by a source meter (Keithley 2400, USA) under simulated AM 1.5 G (100 mW/cm2 irradiance) provided by a solar simulator (Newport Oriel Sol 3A Class AAA, 64023A). The silicon solar cells (80 × 35 mm2) were purchased from Ningbo Angui Electronics Co, Ltd. When measuring single-layer LSCs, the effective area of the silicon solar cell was 25 × 1 mm2, and the V
oc , J
sc , PCE, and EQE of the silicon cell were 0.48 V, 41.28 mA/cm2, 14%, and 16%, respectively. When measuring tandem LSCs, the effective area of the silicon solar cell was 25 × 2 mm2, and the V
oc , J
sc , PCE, and EQE of the silicon cell were 0.54 V, 30 mA/cm2, 11%, and 16%, respectively. During the test, the excess part of the solar cells was covered with black matte tape. The surrounding background of the test was black and no reflectors were used. All the tests were repeated three times for each sample.

The most evident feature of a LSC is its geometric gain (G), a ratio of the top surface area (A
top ) relative to the edge surface area (A
edge ) of the LSC, which is dependent on the dimensions of an LSC:
G =
A top
A
e d g e

The external optical efficiency (η
opt ) is defined as the ratio of the photons coming out of the edge of the LSC relative to the photons hitting on the top surface. In our case, an LSC is characterized with a PV cell attached, and η
opt can be simply calculated as [26,27]:
η opt
=
I LSC
I SC
* G
where I
LSC is the short-circuit current of the PV cell attached to the LSC and I
SC is the short-circuit current measured for the same PV cell under direct illumination with the same light source.

The solid-state SiCDs composites were synthesized solvothermal by heating the mixture of CA, urea, and APTES in H2O/EtOH solvent. By adjusting the precursors to an appropriate proportion, the composites with blue, green, yellow, and orange emission can be obtained (Fig. 1 a). Most of the reported CDs-based composites were obtained by integrating the as prepared CDs into solid matrix, and they only exhibited similar emission as their pure CDs solution [28–30]. The CDs and organosilicon matrix in the SiCDs composites prepared here are formed in situ, and it is easy to tune the optical properties of the SiCDs composites just by controlling the ratios of the precursors. The SiCDs powders change from light yellow to deep yellow under natural light, and red-shifted from blue to yellow under 365 nm UV light as the molar ratio of urea/CA increases from 0 to 32 (the molar ratio of APTES/CA = 6). The emission of SiCDs can further tune to orange by decreasing the molar ratio of APTES/CA = 2 as the molar ratio of urea/CA = 32. The emission peaks are located at 448 nm, 518 nm, 555 nm, and 585 nm, giving corresponding colors of blue, green, yellow, and orange, respectively (Fig. 1b). According to the emissive colors, the SiCDs composites are labelled as B-SiCDs, G-SiCDs, Y-SiCDs, and O-SiCDs, respectively.

To gain insights into the possible origins of different PL emission of the SiCDs composites, the morphology of the composites and the size of the CDs in the SiCDs were first characterized by FESEM and HRTEM. Fig. 2 a–d shows that the morphology of the composite gradually changes from disorderly and high crosslinking agglomeration to regular and low crosslinking spherical shape as the emission of SiCDs red-shifts from blue to orange. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2e–h) reveal that CDs in the composite are uniformly dispersed, with average particle sizes around 3.13, 3.21, 3.37, and 4.19 nm for B-, G-, Y-, and O-SiCDs, respectively, indicating that the size of CDs has a negligible effect on the emission wavelength of SiCDs composites [31]. The corresponding insert images show the circular shape of the CDs with visible crystalline lattice fringes. The lattice spacings of the four CDs are all 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphite carbon [32]. The P-XRD patterns (Fig. 2i) of all the SiCDs exhibit broad peaks at around 23.3°, indicating that the composites are composed of amorphous polysiloxane [33].

The chemical structure and elemental composition of B-, G-, Y-, and O-SiCDs were further studied. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2j) show that the four composites have similar chemical structures. The strong and broad stretching vibrations at 3687-3137 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of –OH and –NH2. The stretching vibration at 1654 cm−1 comes from the C=O groups [34]. The presence of signals from C=O, N–H, and C–H bonds suggests the formation of –CONH between APTES and carbon core, which indicates that the CDs are embedded in the organosilicon with chemical bond [35]. It should be noted that there is an obvious broad absorption between 947 and 1171 cm−1 corresponding to the signal of Si–O–Si and Si–O–C bonds, which indicates the formation of siloxane groups by hydrolysis and condensation reaction [36,37]. The survey of XPS spectrum (Fig. S5) reveals the four composites contain carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and silicon (Si) elements. In the high-resolution scans (Fig. S6), the C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which are C=O (286.9 eV), C–Si (284.8 eV) and C–C (283.8 eV), respectively [38]. The N 1s spectrum exhibits the existence of N–C (399.9 eV) and N–H (398.4 eV) [20]. It is evident that the Si 2p band mainly consists of two peaks, which can be attributed to Si–O (101.8 eV) and Si–C (101.2 eV), respectively. The 13C solid-state NMR spectra (Fig. S7) of four SiCDs show significant chemical shift peaks at 10–60 ppm and 160–175 ppm. The peaks at 10–60 ppm mainly come from sp3 hybrid C in CA and APTES while the peaks at 160–175 ppm come from sp2 hybrid C in CA and urea. The peak at ∼175 ppm corresponds to the carbonyl in CA. The four SiCDs all have a chemical shift peak at ∼165 ppm, while G, Y and O-SiCDs show a new peak at ∼160 ppm. Considering that the electronegativity of the N atom is greater than that of the C atom, the peak at ∼165 ppm can be attributed to the amide bond formed by carboxyl group of CA and amino group of APTES. Therefore, the peak at ∼160 ppm is derived from sp2 hybrid C in urea, which also corresponds to the absence of chemical shift peak at 160 ppm of B-SiCDs without the addition of urea. The above results indicate that the different feeding ratio of APTES, CA, and urea has an effect on the chemical structure of SiCDs. In addition, the CDs are anchored in the “Si–O–Si” network structure formed by the hydrolysis of APTES, and the non-radiative transition is inhibited due to their limited movement, thus achieving the excellent solid-state fluorescence.

The optical properties of the four SiCDs composites are shown in Fig. 3 . For B-, G-, Y-, and O-SiCDs, their PL emission peaks display a slight redshift when the excitation wavelength changes, exhibiting excitation-independent emission (Fig. 3a–d). The optimal excitation wavelengths of B-, G-, Y-, and O-SiCDs are 400, 400, 470 and 470 nm respectively, and the corresponding emission wavelengths are 448, 518, 555 and 585 nm, respectively. The absolute PLQYs for B-, G-, Y-, and O-SiCDs are determined to be 56%, 44%, 32%, and 21% respectively, using an integrating sphere under their optimal excitation wavelengths. The time resolved PL decay curves were also measured and the results are well fitted with a double-exponential formula (Fig. S8, Table S2). The lifetime of SiCDs first increase from 6.73 ns to 11.74 ns, and then decrease to 1.83 ns as the emission red shift. The four composites have significant absorption peaks at 202 nm and 300–350 nm (Fig. 3e–h), which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C bond and n-π* transition of C=O, C–N bond, respectively [39–41]. It is evident that the absorption of the four samples from UV to visible range is gradually enhanced with the red shift of the emission wavelength, which is not seen in the similar CDs/organosilicon composite system [31,33,42]. The above results indicate that the SiCDs prepared here have adjustable solid-state optical properties, which is expected to constructed highly efficient tandem structure LSCs with the SiCDs as the only luminescent materials.

As CDs-based composites, the fluorescence is not only determined by the emission of CD itself, but also by the influence of matrix material on CDs and the interaction among CDs. In our case, the “Si–O–Si” network structure is undoubtedly an important factor affecting the fluorescence of the composite, so the four SiCDs were etched with HF solution [33,43]. The B-, G-, Y-, and O-SiCDs with the same concentration of 10 mg mL−1 were treated with 1% HF solution, and the original turbid suspension becomes clear and transparent after etching (Fig. S9a1-d4). The emission spectra show that the fluorescence of their etching solution experienced different degrees of blue shift compared with that before etching (Figs. S9e–h). Considering that some CDs have been reported to exhibit concentration dependent fluorescence, these etching solutions were further diluted with deionized water at different multiples until the position of emission peak don't change any more (Fig. S10). Eventually, the dilution ratio of B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs can reach 5, 20, 80 and 320 times, respectively, which indicate that the loading rate of CDs in the SiCDs composites increase as the emission red shift from blue to orange. In addition, the fluorescence of the dilute solution finally red shifted from 430 nm, 444 nm, 475 nm–490 nm (Fig. 4 a1-d2 and e), respectively, which may be caused by increased nitrogen doping in CDs that were eventually wrapped in polysiloxane formed by APTES, since the molar ratio of urea to APTES in the feed was gradually increased [44]. According to the variation of fluorescence concentration of diluent, the aggregation state of CDs in polysiloxane can be identified as an important factor affecting emission redshift. In the polysiloxane matrix, increasing CDs loads exacerbate resonance energy transfer (RET) or reabsorption between CDs, eventually leading to a red shift in fluorescence from blue to orange [45]. The concentration dependence of the fluorescence of the SiCDs composites were further investigated (Fig. 4f1-i2 and j, Fig. S11). Interestingly, the fluorescence of dilute aqueous solution of B-SiCDs and G-SiCDs is basically consistent with that of the powder, while the fluorescence of dilute aqueous solution of Y-SiCDs and O-SiCDs is slightly blue shifted compared with that of the powder. This indicates that the loading fraction of CDs in single B-SiCDs particle is low, and there is no significant interaction among CDs in SiCDs particle. However, there are more CDs loaded in G-SiCDs, Y-SiCDs and O-SiCDs, and under the influence of RET effect, the fluorescence of G, Y and O-SiCDs experienced obvious red shift compared with that of CDs. In particular, since Y and O-SiCDs contain more CDs, their powders will further produce red shift in the aggregated state [33,46]. Moreover, it can find that the dilute solution of B-SiCDs has longer emission wavelength (448 nm) than that of their etched dilute solution (430 nm), which can demonstrate that organosilicon matrix cross-linked and coated the CDs also have the influence on the solid-state emission of SiCDs.

According to the above characterization and analysis, Scheme 1 was proposed to illustrate the formation of differences morphology and fluorescence property of the four SiCDs composites under different reaction conditions. At the beginning of the reaction, when the precursor molecules are evenly mixed in the solvent system, the urea and the CA form the reaction center due to the hydrogen bonds between their amino groups and carboxyl groups, while a part of APTES begin to occur hydrolysis and polycondensation reaction to form the low-molecular-weight polysiloxane around the reaction center (Scheme ai-di). For B-SiCDs system, there are relatively fewer reaction centers, and some prepolymers fail to assemble with the reaction center and dissociate around the reaction center. With the increase of urea content in G-SiCDs and Y-SiCDs systems, the increase of reaction centers leads to the gradual reduction of free prepolymers. Although O-SiCDs system and Y-SiCDs system contain the same number of reaction centers molecules, the reduction of APTES means that the reaction centers will be more densely assembled. Under solvothermal conditions, the dehydration process between CA and urea takes place to form CDs, while the prepolymer is further hydrolyzed and condensed to form polysiloxane, beginning to wrap CDs (Scheme aii-dii). In this process, due to the existence of a certain amount of free prepolymers among the reaction centers of B-SiCDs and G-SiCDs system, they will have a direct hydrolysis-condensation reaction with the nearby reaction center, so as to connect the reaction centers together, and eventually lead to the cross-linked morphology of B-SiCDs and G-SiCDs. For Y-SiCDs and O-SiCDs systems, the reaction centers gradually increase and the free prepolymers relatively gradually decrease, resulting in less hydrolyzation-polycondensation reaction between each reaction center, thus presenting a microsphere-like structure (Scheme aiii-diii). Besides the morphology of the composites, it can also utilize the nitrogen doping and increasing CDs content to control the emission of the composites due to the RET between CDs caused by decreasing distance between CDs.

Considering that the SiCDs possess controllable solid-state fluorescence and width absorption spectra from 300 to 550 nm to overlap of the solar spectrum, the SiCDs were further applied to construct tandem LSCs for more effectively capturing sunlight. In order to increase the mixed content of SiCDs as much as possible on the premise of obtaining uniform LSCs, the content of each SiCDs composite were optimized. It is found that the maximum content of B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs is 20 wt%, 15 wt%,15 wt% and 15 wt%, respectively, and these SiCDs-based LSCs are labelled as B-, G-, Y- and O-LSC, respectively.

Fig. 5 a shows the optical photos of B-, G-, Y- and O-LSCs. The words at the bottom of the LSCs can be clearly seen under natural light while their bright fluorescence can be seen under UV light. Transmission spectrum (Fig. 5b) show that the transmittance of these four LSCs is less than 20% in the range of 400–700 nm, which may be caused by the scattering due to the large particle size (∼2 μm) of SiCDs [20,47,48]. Photographs of LSCs taken against a distant building in the background (Fig. S12) show that they are more similar to frosted glass, also indicating the presence of scattering. Absorption spectra (Fig. 5c) shows that the absorbance of B-, G-, Y- and O-LSC increased successively, which is also consistent with the enhanced absorption of the SiCDs powder. The fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 5d) shows that the emission peaks of B-, G-, Y- and O-LSCs are at 448, 516, 556 and 580 nm, respectively, which are basically consistent with the corresponding emission peaks of SiCDs powder. Under different excitation wavelengths, the surface emission of the four LSCs shows non-excitation dependent emission similar to that of the powder without obvious peak deformation (Fig. S13), indicating that exploiting SiCDs as fluorescent material for the preparation of LSCs can effectively overcome the reabsorption and fluorescence quenching caused by CDs in the aggregated state. The emission spectra of B-LSC and G-LSC are slightly redshifted as the excitation wavelength approaches the emission wavelength of the powder, which may be caused by Mie scattering [49]. For B-, G-, Y- and O-LSCs, their fluorescence decay lifetimes are 5.82, 9.93, 6.30 and 2.31 ns, respectively, and their quantum yields are 54%, 42%, 36% and 24%, respectively (Fig. S14, Table S3). Those optical properties of LSCs are very close to those of the SiCDs powder, indicating that SiCDs will not introduce additional non-radiative transition channels even when LSCs are prepared by mixing them in self-made PDMS with high content.

The device performances of LSCs were further measured by irradiating the surface of the LSC with simulated sunlight and coupling a silicon solar cell on one side of the LSCs. The cross-section of quenched LSCs taken by FESEM (Fig. S15) shows that the thickness of the four LSCs is about 1 mm. Therefore, a PV cell with active area of 25 × 1 mm2 was coupled to the edge to measure the photocurrent parameters. Fig. S16 shows the typical photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) characteristic curves of the B-, G-, Y- and O- LSCs. The J
SC of these LSCs all increases with the increase of SiCDs content, and finally reaches 13.15, 9.58, 10.99 and 7.30 mA/cm2, respectively (Fig. 5e). The corresponding external optical efficiencies (η
opt ) are 5.10%, 3.71%, 4.26% and 2.83%, respectively (The photovoltaic parameters such as J
SC , V
OC , FF, G, and η
opt are listed in Tables S4–7).

In order to further evaluate the optical efficiency, the internal quantum efficiency (η
int ) of the LSCs is measured using an integrating sphere. The four edges of the LSCs are covered with black tape to measure their PLQYsurface, then according to the following formula [22,50]:
η
e d g e
=
P L Q
Y
t o t a l
− P L Q
Y
s u r f a c e
P L Q
Y
t o t a l
η int
=
P L Q Y
t o t a l
×
η
e d g e
Where the η
edge is the edge-emission efficiency and the PLQYtotal is the PLQY of the LSCs. The η
int of the B-, G-, Y- and O-LSC can be acquired for 16.8%, 9.8%, 13.0% and 8.0%, respectively (Table S8), indicating that B- and Y-LSCs have less optical loss than G- and O-LSCs, thus exhibiting higher η
opt .

In addition, the effect of scattering on device efficiency cannot be ignored. To assess the effects of scattering, large-area LSCs (50 × 50 × 1 mm3) were prepared and their J−V characteristics were measured (Fig. S17). The η
opt of the B-, G, Y-, and O-LSCs LSCs was 2.94%, 2.33%, 2.63%, 1.48%, which was 43%, 37%, 38%, and 48% lower than that of small-area ones (25 × 25 × 1 mm3), respectively (The photovoltaic parameters such as J
SC
, V
OC
, FF, G, and η
opt are listed in Table S9). This indicates that when the size of LSCs is small, the scattering caused by “Si–O–Si” structure is conducive to guiding photons towards the edge of LSCs, thus enhancing the efficiency of LSCs.

Then, the four LSCs with the optimize content of SiCDs (B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs of 20 wt%, 15 wt%,15 wt% and 15 wt%, respectively) were stacked together in the form of “short-wavelength emitting at the top and long-wavelength emitting at the bottom” (Fig. 6 a), and their edges were fixed with transparent epoxy resin. Six tandem LSCs were constructed, and they were labelled as BG-, BY-, BO-, GY-, GO- and YO-LSC respectively according to the single layer LSCs composed of them. Fig. 6b shows the photo of the B-LSC stacked on the Y-LSC, clearly showing the fluorescence emitted from their respective edges. By measuring the J-V characteristic curves of the six tandem LSCs (Fig. 6c), it can be found that their J
SC are further enhanced and more efficient than the single LSCs comprising them (The photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table S10). In particular, the η
opt of BY-LSCs is the highest, reaching 7.10%, which is comparable to some reported tandem LSCs based on quantum dots or conventional CDs [51,52]. The high efficiency of the BY-LSC in this study is mainly attributed to the following reasons: (1) B-SiCDs and Y-SiCDs possess moderate solid-state PLQY compared to other CDs [53], so they can efficiently absorb sunlight without suffering fluorescence loss caused by quenching even at high mixed content (20 wt% and 15 wt%, respectively). (2) The absorption of Y- SiCDs at 300–500 nm is significantly increased compared with that of B-SiCDs, and when sunlight irradiates BY-LSC, part of the light is absorbed by the upper B-LSC, while the transmitted light will be absorbed by the lower Y-LSC, so as to make full use of sunlight in tandem structure. (3) The micron size of B-SiCDs and Y-SiCDs can effectively scatter incident light and fluorescence to the edge of LSCs, thus improving the efficiency of LSCs. Moreover, after these single-layer LSCs and tandem LSCs were stored at normal temperature and pressure for more than four months, the external optical efficiency remained above 88% of the initial condition (Fig. S18, Table S11), showing good long-term service performance.

In summary, we have demonstrated the synthesis of SiCDs with multi-color emission from blue to orange by adjusting the ratio of APTES/CA or urea/CA to control the content of CDs in the composite and the morphology of SiCDs. The SiCDs display moderate solid-state PLQY, which apply to fabricated single-layer LSCs by B-, G-, Y- and O-SiCDs with high mixed content. Further with the beneficial of scattering effect, the external optical efficiencies of the four single-layer LSCs finally reach 5.10%, 3.71%, 4.26% and 2.83%, respectively. Furthermore, the external optical efficiency of the tandem structured LSCs based on B-LSC and Y-LSC reaches up to 7.10%, which greatly increase the use of solar energy. This work not only provides a new idea for the synthesis of CDs/organosilicon composites with controllable solid-state fluorescence, but also broadens the application prospect of CDs/organosilicon composites in LSCs.

Yuhan Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Liuxi Zhao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiyue Cao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuhong Zhang: Investigation. Xuanfeng Jiang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhengguang Sun: Formal analysis, Supervision. Yuan Zhan: Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work is financially supported by the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021CFB025) and the Oversea Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:para:

Mater., 28 (2016) 312-318, https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201503380.
